sources.notes: Read the full statement and constraints: 0 <= l,r < 2^30 and r-l>1; three distinct values in [l,r] must maximize the sum of their three pairwise XORs.
Official Hello 2025 editorial implementation; confirms the highest-differing-bit boundary construction a=l|((1<<k)-1), b=a+1, and a distinct endpoint as c.
Consulted for the per-bit contribution argument and a materially different construction that fixes l and r and greedily builds the third number bit by bit.
Independently proved the per-bit upper bound and boundary-pair optimality; exhaustively checked both constructions on all 1,953 intervals 0<=l<r<64 with r-l>1.

reference_candidates.notes: Additional Luogu solution index; not needed beyond the consulted article.

annotation.summary: For each interval [l,r], output three pairwise distinct integers that maximize (a xor b)+(b xor c)+(a xor c).
annotation.constraints: Up to 10^4 tests; 0<=l,r<2^30 and r-l>1, so a fixed 30-bit construction is required.
annotation.core_idea: Let k be the most significant set bit of l xor r. Every value in [l,r] has the same prefix above k, so those bits can never contribute. At each bit i<=k, the three pairwise XORs contribute either 0 (all three bits equal) or exactly 2*2^i (both bit values occur), giving the upper bound 2^(k+2)-2. Set a=l|((1<<k)-1), the largest interval value with bit k=0, and b=a+1, the smallest with bit k=1. Then a and b are complementary on every bit 0..k, so every relevant bit reaches its upper bound regardless of the third value. Choose c=l if a!=l, otherwise c=r; r-l>1 guarantees distinctness.
annotation.complexity: O(log C) time per test to locate the highest differing bit (O(1) for the fixed 30-bit domain), O(1) memory.
tricks: Decompose the objective independently by bit.
Use the highest bit where l and r differ to identify the only contributing suffix.
Choose the adjacent numbers on opposite sides of that binary boundary; their suffixes are complements.
Once two numbers differ at every relevant bit, any valid distinct third number preserves the optimum.

solution_variants.name: boundary-complement-pair
solution_variants.summary: Official construction: find k=msb(l xor r), choose the largest value a with the common prefix and bit k=0, choose b=a+1, then use a distinct endpoint as c. The pair a,b alone forces maximum contribution at every possible bit.

solution_variants.name: fixed-endpoints-bit-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Fix two team members as l and r. For every low bit where l and r agree, force the third number's bit to the opposite value; for bits where they differ, greedily choose bits while maintaining l<c<r. This also makes every bit up to msb(l xor r) nonconstant.

tags.evidence: k=msb(l xor r) determines the common immutable prefix and the full suffix whose per-bit upper bounds can be saturated.
The task is solved by explicitly constructing the optimal boundary pair and a guaranteed distinct third value, with no search over triples.
The proof and construction operate on binary bits, using l xor r, suffix masks, and complementary bit patterns.
The alternative construction fixes l,r and chooses the third number's bits from high to low while preserving its interval bounds.
A per-bit counting argument proves the global upper bound 2^(k+2)-2 and certifies that the construction attains it.
